items.q: What distinguishes gamma rays from X-rays of the same frequency?
items.correct: Origin — gamma rays come from nuclear processes; X-rays come from electron transitions or deceleration of charged particles.
items.misconception: Gamma rays have higher frequency than any X-ray.
items.random_wrong: Gamma rays are slower than X-rays.
items.why: Students distinguish bands by frequency exclusively, missing that gamma and X-ray bands overlap in frequency and are classified by origin.

items.q: If a car has negative acceleration, what does that necessarily mean?
items.correct: Its acceleration is in the negative direction (often opposite to the chosen positive direction); it may be slowing down or speeding up in reverse.
items.misconception: Negative acceleration always means the car is slowing down.
items.random_wrong: Negative acceleration means the car is moving backwards.
items.why: Students conflate 'deceleration' with 'negative acceleration'; sign indicates direction relative to the chosen axis, not necessarily slowing.

items.correct: The stiffness of a material — the ratio of stress to strain in the elastic region.
items.misconception: Young's modulus measures how strong a material is — how much force it can take before breaking.
items.random_wrong: Young's modulus measures the density of a material.
items.why: Students conflate stiffness (resistance to deformation) with strength (resistance to failure) because both feel like 'tough'.
It's Position and momentum. “Mass and charge” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Probability density. “Energy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If Δx = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m, minimum Δp (kg m/s, ℏ = 1.05e-34):
items.why: The real figure is 5.3e-25. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
